text: The video shows the surgical field for a transabdominal preperitoneal repair of a left-sided indirect inguinal hernia. A visible scar is present on the right side from a previous surgery. The surgeon identifies the deep inguinal ring on the left side as the target for dissection.

The surgeon uses a supraumbilical port due to the patient's height, positioning the camera port at a 24-centimeter arc from the hernia site. The previous surgical scar is visible. The setup includes two working instruments positioned at 18 centimeters with lateral spacing of 7.5 centimeters for the left and right hand ports, following the baseball diamond concept. A telescope is used for visualization.

The surgeon prepares for the procedure by creating a small stab wound at the camera port using a scalpel. The varus needle is inserted with the left hand, followed by suction irrigation and the hanging drop test to confirm entry into the abdominal cavity. Once verified, the tubing of the insufflator is attached to begin pneumoperitoneum with a preset pressure of fifteen.

The initial gas flow rate is set to one liter per minute, then increased to three liters per minute. Once the preset pressure of fifteen is reached, the homogeneous distance is achieved, and the varus needle is removed. The stab wound is enlarged from two millimeters to ten millimeters, and the trocar is introduced using a screwing motion, held like a pistol with the middle finger wrapped around the air inlet. The gas leakage through the trocar is checked, and the insufflator is reattached with the flow rate set to a maximum of ten liters per minute.

The optical trocar is inserted, and the telescope is introduced to inspect the entry point for any inadvertent injury. The surgeon checks the hernia site, confirming everything is intact. Secondary ports are placed 7.5 centimeters lateral from the 18-centimeter arc, with the left-hand port being 5 millimeters. The placement is done under vision, ensuring careful avoidance of the inferior epigastric vessel.

The surgeon positions the 7.5 cm port ipsilaterally, with the right hand using a harmonic scalpel and the left hand using a grasper to begin the surgery. The operating room light is dimmed to 20 lux. The left-sided indirect inguinal hernia is visible, along with the inferior epigastric vessel, medial umbilical ligament, triangle of doom, and triangle of pain.

The surgeon begins dissection from the medial umbilical ligament, positioned just above the arc of the hernia defect. The dissection proceeds six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the defect at the ten o'clock position, following the ipsilateral approach for left-sided hernia repair.

At the ten o'clock position, the surgeon carefully dissects near the inferior epigastric vessel using a harmonic device, pushing the vessel towards the anterior abdominal wall. The dissection proceeds six centimeters laterally, preparing for mesh placement. A pre-peritoneal incision is made, focusing on dissecting the triangle of pain before addressing the hernia sac.

The surgeon performs a blunt dissection of the triangle of pain, breaking away areolar tissue to create a pocket for a large mesh placement. The lateral peritoneal dissection is completed, ensuring only the peritoneum is cut while minimizing the use of energy sources.

The surgeon identifies the medial boundary of the triangle of pain by locating the spermatic vessel, confirming the completion of lateral dissection. The procedure progresses to medial dissection, targeting the trapezoid of disaster area and the space of radius, with the inferior epigastric vessel and medial umbilical ligament being pushed medially.

The surgeon uses both hands to separate the bladder and identify the Cooper's ligament, which appears pearly white and reflective. The Cooper's ligament is pushed inferiorly and medially, revealing the retropubic space known as the space of radius. The urinary bladder and sigmoid colon are visible in the surgical field, with the shiny Cooper's ligament clearly defined.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection and proceeds to dissect the hernia sac. The left hand pulls the peritoneum while the right hand pushes the pseudo sac, which is the transversalis fascia. In the case of an indirect hernia, the peritoneum is carefully separated from the vas deferens and spermatic vessel, allowing for the sac to be extracted.

The surgeon identifies the indirect hernia defect, which is lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel and above the inguinal ligament. The sac is pulled with one hand while the spermatic vessel is visible laterally and the vas deferens medially. The surgeon carefully separates the sac, pushing the transversalis fascia back into the defect, avoiding pulling the pseudo sac. A visible fat pad is present at the neck of the sac, and other structures are gently pushed away.

The surgeon carefully dissects the hernia sac, identifying the inferior epigastric vessel above, the vas deferens medially, and the spermatic vessel laterally. The transversalis fascia, referred to as the pseudo sac, is visible above the sac. The surgeon lifts the lower flap to enter the defect from outside, assessing the extent of dissection and ensuring a sufficient pocket is created for placing a large mesh.

The surgeon uses the right hand to push the transversalis fascia back while the left hand holds the hernia sac. Care is taken to avoid creating a buttonhole, which would prevent proper peritoneal coverage over the mesh. The peritoneum is visible, and the vas deferens and spermatic vessel are being separated. The sac is successfully extracted, and the transversalis fascia is repositioned, with no visible holes remaining, indicating successful peritoneal coverage.

The surgeon separates the sac from the transversalis fascia, revealing extra peritoneum that aids in mesh coverage. The vas deferens and spermatic vessels are visible medially as they are carefully cleared during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the spermatic vessel and vas deferens, avoiding the creation of a buttonhole. The triangle of pain and triangle of doom areas are visible, with the iliac vessels clearly seen between them. The peritoneum is positioned below these structures.

The surgeon uses two atraumatic graspers to perform blunt dissection, pushing the spermatic vessel away from the peritoneum to enlarge the surgical pocket. The vas deferens is visible medially, and the triangle of doom is widened as the peritoneum is separated from it.

The surgeon stands near the right shoulder with the monitor positioned near the left shoulder. The surgical field includes the triangle of doom and triangle of pain, with visible epigastric vessels and Cupid's ligaments. The vas deferens and spermatic vessels are clearly identified, and the space of radius is expanded. The trapezoid of disaster is carefully examined, and the iliac vessels are visible with pulsations.

The surgeon demonstrates the anatomy of the spermatic vessel, which is slightly pulled away, and the iliac vessel, highlighting the external iliac artery and vein. The vein is positioned medially while the artery is lateral. The space between the external iliac artery and vein is visible, showing the pulsation of the external iliac artery. The dissection is complete, with clear visualization of the external iliac vessel, vas deferens, and spermatic vessel.

The surgeon has formed the space of Bogros and the space of Retzius, with the inferior epigastric vessel visible. The hernia sac is completely removed, and the pocket around the defect is formed with a six-centimeter margin. The peritoneum over the vas deferens is pulled down, completing the dissection in the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster.

The surgeon identifies the space of radius, noting the presence of the coronamortis vein and potential aberrant obturator vessel. The bladder area and the tenderness arc of levator ani, visible as a white line below the Cooper ligament, are also identified. The inferior epigastric vessel, spermatic vessel, and vas deferens are clearly visible. The surgical pocket is confirmed to be firm, and the surgeon prepares to place a transabdominal preperitoneal mesh, sized twelve plus the defect. The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve of the thigh and the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve are visible.

The surgeon positions the mesh into the pocket laterally, ensuring it covers the defect adequately. The mesh is unfolded to spread evenly, with its center aligned with the defect's center. The first fixation is performed using a titanium tacker at the Cooper's ligament, visible as a white structure at the inferior medial end.

The surgeon fixes the mesh laterally onto the transversus abdominis muscle, followed by peritonization to cover the defect. The gas pressure is reduced to 8-12 mmHg to facilitate the covering process. Care is taken to avoid disturbing the sigmoid colon during this step. The tacker is used to secure the mesh, ensuring it is fired perpendicular to the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon confirms that the entire defect is covered, ensuring no visible mesh or defect remains. Care is taken to avoid injury to the inferior epigastric vessel during the firing of the tacker. As the gas is released, the peritoneum naturally adheres to the underlying structures, completing the repair of the left side indirect inguinal hernia using the transabdominal preperitoneal approach.
